paragraphs.body: Turning to walk up the stairs, I grab my last remaining bags and glance around the four walls that were my safe place and start to feel a little sad as I turn to retreat down the stairs, I meet my father's curious eyes. I know he feels my excitement but discomfort as we both dance around each other saying nothing at all. Growing up college was it, we both sat and looked through brochures trying to figure out what school would prepare me the best for my expectations and dreams.

annotations.comment: Notice that in the same sentence she both walked up and retreated down the stairs. :)
annotations.context_left: le sad as I turn to

annotations.comment: Like the idea that this is a dream they shared — powerful to think about how that has changed.

paragraphs.body: My dad was and still if I allowed it would be my biggest supporter but after so much disappointment and choices, he made that ruined my childhood I attended the school that never made it on the list. I reached down to grab the suitcase about to explode from the seams and hand it to my dad's awaiting arms. I grabbed a couple loose bags and followed him out of my room.

annotations.comment: Chose the school?

paragraphs.body: I bound down the stairs and out the front door to the beat-up station wagon that used to be my grandmother's and threw the last bag in. The entire trunk of the station wagon is packed. I am already dreading unloading all of this by myself when I get there.

Walking around to the driver’s seat I sat and start to connect the aux and navigation to Georgia the next seven hours with just my thoughts alone creates a pit in my stomach. My father starts tapping on the window.

Rolling the window down, “Hey Mckenna, I just want you to know I’m so proud of you and you are going to do great at college.” I can feel that he wants to say more but simply says “text me when you get there safely,”
	Giving him a small smile, “Thanks Dad I will.”
	I rolled the window up, made a small wave and finally put the car into drive. I make one last peak through the rearview mirror seeing my dad waving, and what it seems like as he wipes a tear from his eye. I swallow the lump in my throat and try to focus on how excited I am, but I can’t help the sadness that washes over me.

annotations.comment: It might be interesting if he tries to say more but she won’t allow him to do so.
annotations.context_right: just want you to know I’m so proud of you and you are going to do great at college.” I can feel tha

paragraphs.body: My college in Georgia is seven hours away from my hometown in Virginia. A perfect distance far away from a town that made me feel trapped. I love the University I chose but the most appealing aspect was the distance. It’s a perfect day in August. The sun is so bright, and the blue sky is stripped with clouds. I think of all the possibilities at my new school, trying not to let self-doubt seep in.

I wish I could say the familiar scenery of my home state calmed me as I drove, or that a sense of an exciting adventure overcame me as I passed each sign indicating that I was getting close to Emory. But really, I was struck in my own head planning and obsessing.

annotations.comment: That feels realistic.

paragraphs.body: As I finally drive through the stone entrance and onto the campus. It looks just as perfect as it did online, and I am immediately impressed with the architecture of the school. Hundreds of people, parents hugging, kissing, crying telling their children goodbye, clusters of new students dressed in our school colors, and a few people who don’t seem like they fit in fill the grounds. The size of the campus makes me a little nervous but hopefully I will start to find my place here and it’ll feel like home.

I find a parking spot and grab my bag as I try to find my freshman orientation. After I listen intently to all they have to say for three hours I finally am free to leave. I used the map they handed me at freshman orientation to find the dorm building I was assigned. After a short walk, my eyes scan an older building that will be my home for the next year. I feel a smile appear on my face as I finally start to feel excitement bubbling inside of me. I get a new pep in my step as I go back to my car to move it closer to the dorm and get my bags.

annotations.comment: It seemed a little off to attend orientation before moving in, but maybe she arrived late? (The timing is strange too - she left in the morning and drove 7 hours, so now it’s, like, 7 or 8 PM?)

paragraphs.body: I pull my car up close to the curb and put my hazards on, I have such a heavy suitcase and since it's only me I want to try to not do as many trips as possible. I am out of breath as I almost lug my hundred-pound suitcase to the sixth floor in hall A. I slide my key, my new student id, that they gave us at first-year students orientation into the old wooden door at the end of a long hallway. When it creaks open, I am greeted by a small room, with two single beds and two desks.

annotations.comment: Yeah, I would just have her move in, without the orientation.

paragraphs.body: My eyes travel to the various girly posters and the light blue comforter, my roommate Layla’s side of the room. She’s not here, I wonder where she is. I lugged my huge suitcase to the center of the room. I turn around to gather the rest of my bags from the car.

An hour later, I had all my bags in my room, and I found my student parking spot for my car. I feel overwhelmed with the amount of stuff I have to unpack by myself. I wish for a moment my dad was here helping me move all this in, I miss before, I miss the days when we did not have this hostility and awkwardness between us, maybe it's my fault? Maybe I am a terrible daughter for the way I can’t ignore what happened and just pretend like our relationship was never affected by his decisions.

annotations.text: my dad was here helping me move
annotations.comment: Yeah, I bet she does!  Nice connection to what she feels she should have but can’t.

paragraphs.body: Whatever, I can’t think like that now, I sit on the floor and start to get to work sorting everything. Shortly, half my clothes are neatly folded and stored in one of the small dressers; and the remainder hung in my closet. I look over to Layla’s side of the room wondering when she will come back,
My thoughts are interrupted by the click of the lock, suggesting someone is here.
“Hey,” she says, offering a smile, a smile that seems so genuine and full of life, she's wearing a tennis skirt and a bright pink top with long brown hair falling to her mid back.
“H-hey. I’m Mckenna,” I barely get out, my ability to speak clearly flying out the window.
“Hey, Mckenna, nice to meet you. I am so excited for this semester all ready. OMG I’ve heard that the parties this week are so fun, it's something they do for orientation week. We have to go!”

She smiles so wide and walks over, closing the gap between us, and wraps her arms around my body. I’m frozen for a moment surprised by her intimate gesture when she doesn’t even know me, but I return her hug.

annotations.comment: I like her! (Have they met online before?)

paragraphs.body: I hear another knock on the door.
“Come in!” Layla yells. The door opens and two boys walk inside.
“Hey, you Layla’s roommate?” the first boy asks. He has blonde hair styled with gel as it sits up.
“Um... yes. My name is Mckenna,” I manage to say, as I am stunned by the second boy. As my eyes connect with the familiar brown eyes of Wyatt Black. What the fuck is he doing here?

annotations.comment: Reader, I gasped! Good introduction.

paragraphs.body: I’m Drew. You feeling, ok? He says with a smile, reaching out to touch my shoulder. His expression is warm and inviting. I nod my head and give him a small smile. Layla interrupts our odd interaction by planting a kiss on Drew’s lips, “Hey babe, finally I’m starving ugh, let’s go I need to grab my bag.”

annotations.comment: I might want to know a little bit about how these two already seem so comfortable at the school and with each other.
annotations.context_left: o I need to grab my

paragraphs.body: I try to get Wyatt’s attention, but he steps back out of the door, “I'll wait out here guys.”
He always stayed to himself in high school from what I gathered he was two years older than me so it’s not like I was around him all the time. I thought he played football somewhere else.

annotations.text: what I gathered he was two years
annotations.comment: Run on

paragraphs.body: “I’m ready, guys,” Layla says, grabbing a heavy blue bag from her bed. “Are you hungry? Do you wanna come Mckenna?”
“I’m ok thanks, I am going to keep organizing,” I am hungry but based off Wyatt’s reaction I don’t think I would be welcome.
“Ok I’ll be back later;” Layla says as the two of them exit the room. I let out a long breath.

I feel exhausted from the day, I lie across the bed. A familiar loneliness creeps its poison into me already. I can’t help but feel regret knowing I should have gone to get food with my new roommate. I feel my stomach grumble as I decide to venture out and find something to eat.

annotations.comment: Let her think about Wyatt a bit before making a plan for the rest of the day.

annotations.text: I can’t help but feel regret knowing I should have gone to get food with my new roommate.
annotations.comment: Oh, I have felt that. Very relatable.

paragraphs.body: I stumble across a subway close to my dorm, grabbing a sub to go, I walk back to my dorm. I set my sandwich down at my desk and go grab my planner and textbooks, taking the time to write down my classes for the semester.

I plan for a trip off campus tomorrow to get some more things and food for my dorm room. I finish my sub and start to get ready for bed in the communal bathroom. Lying in bed I try to focus on plans, school, schedules, but drift off to the brown eyes of Wyatt Black.

annotations.comment: This feels a little sad — not in a bad way, I was just feeling for this girl.

paragraphs.body: The next morning, Layla is not in her bed. I would like to get to know her, but that might be difficult if she’s never around. I know Drew is her boyfriend from the few messages back and forth over the summer. Grabbing my toiletry bag, I make my way to the communal bathroom. I can already tell this is going to be my least favorite thing about dorm life. I wish each room had its own bathroom.

annotations.comment: Go to dinner with her!
Maybe this could tell us more — he’s an upperclassman, or something?

paragraphs.body: I get to the shower and it's a tiny stall, with a hook to hang my towel. I have barely enough room to stretch my arms in front of me. I find my mind drifting back to my dad and life at home. Distracted, I turn around and knock my clothes off the hook completely soaking them.

“You’ve got to be kidding me!” I groan to myself, wrap my towel around my body and grab my soaked clothes rushing down the hall. I reach my room and shove my ID in hoping no one sees how embarrassing this is. Until my eyes meet Layla and the two boys from last night.

“Umm... Hi guys” trying to sound normal but my voice comes out high-pitched and fake. My hands grabbing the soft fabric of my towel, and my eyes keep glancing down to make sure it’s covering my naked body.

annotations.comment: Great moment.

paragraphs.body: Wyatt looks at me, the corners of his mouth lifting slightly, but doesn’t say a word.
Layla, interrupts our whatever that was, “OMG, are you ok why are your clothes soaked? Guys get out she obviously needs to change I’ll text you later about tonight.”

The boys both look concerned and leave the room without a word. “Haha, thanks, I had an accident in the shower, they don’t make those stalls big enough at all, and I knocked all my clothes to the floor.”

annotations.comment: I would have her reveal the clothes got soaked here, and not have McKenna guess that.

paragraphs.body: “Oh my god, no!! That is terrible I hate communal bathrooms so much.”

I walk over to my dresser and put on a bra and panties, followed by a light pink shirt and jean shorts. Layla asks from behind me, “What are your plans for the day?” as she applies mascara to her eyelashes. I told her about my plan to pick up some dorm essentials and snacks. “Oh, that’s a good idea, there is a party tonight; you should come with us!”

annotations.comment: Unclear — Is she inviting herself to go get snacks too?

paragraphs.body: I give her an awkward smile unaware if she can see me through the reflection of her mirror, “parties aren’t really my thing. I was going to decorate my room with the stuff I bought earlier for my side of the room.”
“C’monnnn... it's a party the first week of college, classes start Monday and then you can do all that responsible stuff! You’re in college now, just one party won’t hurt,” she begs with puppy eyes.

Desperate to fit in and make a new friend even if it's not how I usually spend my Friday nights, I hear myself respond, “Ok you’ve convinced me, I laugh, falling victim to her bubbly energy and puppy eyes, “It sounds like it might be fun.”

annotations.comment: Grammar and punctuation are a little messy throughout. I do think that she probably would say yes more easily, given that she just was thinking about how she needs to make friends and get to know Layla.

paragraphs.body: Layla squeals before wrapping her arms around me in a tight hug. “Yay! We’ll have so much fun! She shrieks. A big part of me is hoping she’ll be right.
After my errands at the store, I grabbed a couple snacks and breakfast bars for the next week. I use my ID to open the door to our dorm and I'm relieved to see Layla sitting on her bed.

I need more details about the party to calm my nerves. “Where is the party? Can we walk there? Or Uber?” I asked calmly, ignoring the anxious fluttering as I put all my snacks away into the bins under my bed.

annotations.comment: I’d have all the dialogue in one scene.
annotations.context_left: I need more details about the party
annotations.context_right: calm my nerves. “Where is the party? Can we walk there? Or Uber?” I asked calmly, ignoring the anxi

paragraphs.body: “Technically, it’s a football party, at one of Drew’s friends' houses. He’s cool when I used to visit Drew, last year, we would always go over he has a really big backyard with a bonfire. The football team can get in trouble with the coach and the school for throwing huge parties this close to the season, so it’ll be more of a chill get together.
“It’s a little off campus but Drew is going to pick us up.”

I’m glad it won’t be Wyatt even though I know he will be there. Riding with him would be awkward considering I’ve gotten the feeling he doesn’t like me. He probably thinks I’m a freak just like everyone else from my hometown.

annotations.comment: Might be helpful to work in a mention of Wyatt being Drew’s best friend, etc.

paragraphs.body: Drew’s going to be here in an hour let’s get ready, you can help me pick out my outfit,” Layla says as she lays out a bunch of cropped tops and skirts on her bed. I smile at everyone but internally cringe as the tops are so short.

annotations.comment: I always enjoy a slightly prudish main character (because I relate!)

paragraphs.body: She settles on a black crop top with a jean skirt, which makes her look flattering, surprisingly. I grabbed a short-sleeved light grey top and some ripped jeans and threw my hair in a high ponytail.

Just as I’m putting on some light makeup, her phone buzzes and she grabs her purse. “Drew’s here,” she says. I grab my sneakers and purse as we make our way around the building to the front where Drew is waiting.

As we got into his sports car, I'm met with an overwhelming ax body spray and rap music cranked to the max. I sat in the backseat and stare out the window, trying to drown out the awful loud music. Finally, Drew parks on the side of a busy street lined with what looks like run down party houses, as red solo cups cover the lawns.

annotations.comment: I continue to enjoy her desire to make friends but maybe not THESE friends?

paragraphs.body: I follow Layla and Drew through one of the houses’ front yards that doesn’t have as much college party trash filling the lawn. As soon as we open the front door, the party is a lot larger than I had previously thought, if this is what Layla considers a small get together, I’m worried about what she considers to be party.

I follow Layla and Drew into a crowded living room and am handed a red solo cup. I didn’t drink a lot in high school, I wasn’t really invited to many parties. I remember sneaking alcohol from my friends' parents liquor cabinets before everything happened. I take a sip from the red solo cup and it's some type of red juice with a bite which I would assume would be the liquor. It’s not that bad as I take a couple more sips.

I continue to follow Layla and Drew as they greet many people at the party and make their way outside to the bonfire. A bunch of guys sit around the bonfire and a couple girls all look over when we arrive.
“This is Mckenna, my roommate. She just got here yesterday so I wanted to introduce you guys to her.” I know Layla did not just formally introduce me to all these people.
I give a small wave and find an empty seat. I knock my knees together and fold in my lips, feeling antsy. Out of place.

One by one they all nod or smile at me. All of them seem so friendly except for Wyatt who I can feel staring through me on the opposite side of the bonfire. I clutch my empty red solo as if it is my lifeline away from his stares.

Wyatt is sitting across from me at a bonfire at Emory. Racking my brain trying to figure out why he is at this party and at this school. I remember people talking about him not liking the coach at his old college.

annotations.comment: This might need to be a more specific memory — she overheard it in the hallway, etc.

paragraphs.body: People always raved about him because he was one of the first football players to go to D1 in fifteen years from our area. Blah Blah, He must have transferred to Emory... shit. I would recognize those eyes anywhere he’s been my secret crush since the fourth grade.

annotations.text: been my secret crush since the fourth grade
annotations.comment: We need this information the minute she first sees him.

paragraphs.body: I used to sit next to him at lunch in middle school. His mom would always write notes hoping that he was having a good day telling him how much she loved him and then I would watch him throw them away.

I was always so envious of him. My mom worked all the time, so I rarely saw her and when I did, she made it seem like it took time out of her day rather than prioritizing me for the little time we had.

Wyatt is seated on the bench across from me, his face going in and out of focus as I try to think about anything else other than the boy across from me. I’ve caught him staring at me a few times and I wonder what he feels when he looks at me. Disenchantment would be my guess, and pity taking second place.

Some big jock is seated beside him, chugging down his fifth beer and acting loud and moronic. He’s living up to the football player stereotype just as effortlessly as I embrace my tragic shy outcast persona, so I can’t really judge his character.

I watch as Wyatt reaches into the cooler beside him before I pull my eyes away and redirect my attention to my hands fisted tightly in my lap. I circle my thumbs zeroing in on my still-chipped nail polish.

I’m so immersed in my fingernails that I fail to notice Wyatt has left his friends side and is now taking a seat to my left. It’s a small bench so our shoulders brush. Warmth seeps through the thick sleeve of my hoodie as his scent surrounds me, peppermint gum mixed in with firewood smoke.

annotations.comment: I think you did a great job of creating a situation that she feels deeply uncomfortable in, which then allows him to try to rescue her, a bit. Strong excerpt, Evelyn. Your pace is a little rushed and sometimes you include things we don’t really need to see (it turned out to not be important that she went to the store, for example) but that can be fixed in subsequent drafts. My only big note here, besides to suggest that you keep writing!, is that you might want the dad to try to be in touch — maybe he texts? And she can decide whether she answers or not. Great work! Happy writing!
